original_question:
Patrick started walking at a constant rate along a straight road from school to the park. One hour after Patrick left, Tanya started running along the same road from school to the park. One hour after Tanya left, Jose started bicycling along the same road from school to the park. Tanya ran at a constant rate of $2$ miles per hour faster than Patrick walked, Jose bicycled at a constant rate of $7$ miles per hour faster than Tanya ran, and all three arrived at the park at the same time. The distance from the school to the park is $\frac{m}{n}$ miles, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Find $m + n$.

Present the answer in LaTex format: \boxed{Your answer}

entries.original_incomplete_question: Sophia started walking at a constant rate along a straight road from school to the park. One hour after Sophia left, Benjamin started running along the same road from school to the park. One hour after Benjamin left, Ava started bicycling along the same road from school to the park. Benjamin ran at a constant rate of 6 miles per hour faster than [BLANK], Ava bicycled at a constant rate of 11 miles per hour faster than Benjamin ran, and all three arrived at the park at the same time. The distance from the school to the park is \frac{m}{n} miles, where m and n are relatively prime positive integers. Find m + n.

variants.variant: Sophia began her walk at a steady pace on a straight path from school to the park. An hour after Sophia departed, Benjamin commenced his run along the same path from school to the park. An hour after Benjamin started, Ava took off on her bicycle along the same route from school to the park. Benjamin's running speed was 6 miles per hour greater than [BLANK], while Ava's cycling speed was 11 miles per hour more than Benjamin's running speed, and all three reached the park simultaneously. The distance from school to the park is  rac{m}{n} miles, where m and n are relatively prime positive integers. Calculate m + n.

Starting from school to the park, Sophia walked at a consistent speed along a direct road. One hour after she set off, Benjamin began his run on the same route from school to the park. An hour following Benjamin's departure, Ava started biking down the same road from school to the park. Benjamin's speed was 6 miles per hour faster than [BLANK], and Ava's biking speed was 11 miles per hour quicker than Benjamin's running pace, with all three arriving at the park at the same moment. The distance between the school and the park is  rac{m}{n} miles, where m and n are relatively prime positive integers. Determine m + n.

Sophia initiated her walk at a uniform pace along a straight road from school toward the park. An hour after her departure, Benjamin started running along that same road from school to the park. One hour after Benjamin left, Ava began her bike ride along the same road from school to the park. The speed at which Benjamin ran was 6 miles per hour more than [BLANK], whereas Ava's speed on her bicycle was 11 miles per hour higher than Benjamin's running speed, and all three arrived at the park concurrently. The distance from the school to the park is  rac{m}{n} miles, where m and n are relatively prime positive integers. Find m + n.

Beginning her journey from school to the park, Sophia walked at a constant speed along a straight route. An hour after she left, Benjamin set off running on the same road from school to the park. One hour after Benjamin's departure, Ava started pedaling along the same road from school to the park. Benjamin's running speed was 6 miles per hour faster than [BLANK], while Ava's cycling speed was 11 miles per hour beyond Benjamin's running speed, and they all arrived at the park at the same time. The distance from the school to the park is  rac{m}{n} miles, where m and n are relatively prime positive integers. Calculate m + n.

The rate at which Benjamin ran was 6 miles per hour more than [BLANK], and Ava's bicycling rate was 11 miles per hour faster than Benjamin's running pace, with all three arriving at the park simultaneously.
